body_txt: Some fights have rules. Most don't. However, a lot of people will still fight as though there are rules. Heh... suckers . The Combat Pragmatist is a character who is defined by their willingness to do whatever it takes to win a fight. This anything-goes mentality is typically (but not always) reserved for fights where there's actually something to lose beyond a cash prize, a trophy, or a title belt. In a professional bout, where safeguards are in place to protect the combatants, and where fighters can be disqualified for flouting the rules, the Combat Pragmatist is an unsympathetic character (usually the main antagonist) who is only in it to win and doesn't give a crap about anything — not their opponent, not the sport and its customs, not the tournament's sacred history — but themselves, and who usually gets taken down a peg in the end . In a fight where the stakes are life-or-death, however, the audience is not likely to see failure to abide by the rules as cheap: the only 'rule' in a fight for survival is to get yourself and your protectorate out alive. The Combat Pragmatist is characterized by an extensive knowledge of tactics others may consider "dirty" fighting or just by a willingness to use those tactics to achieve their goal, often against more "honorable" opponents. They are likely to think of any adherence to rules or sporting combat as Honor Before Reason . They do not suffer from a Complexity Addiction , are never afraid to use a Mundane Solution (nor do they need others to point it out to them ), never assume that the other person will fight honorably, have no interest in handicapping themselves , don't care if their opponent is a worthy one , and will not hesitate to Kick Them While They Are Down . They almost always have their weapon of choice either on them or near them at all times, and possibly a spare or two: knives and daggers are an especially popular choice for such characters due to their light weight and ease of concealment. So do guns , especially if everybody else is adverse to carrying firearms for whatever reason . But if caught without their weapons, they're willing to use absolutely anything as a weapon and only resort to Good Old Fisticuffs if there's no weapon nearby. They will not hesitate to use a Dangerous Forbidden Technique . They have no compunction about hurting a child . Or about attacking someone from behind . Or throwing sand in their eyes . Or pulling down their pants (or flipping up their skirts if dealing with women or a kilt-wearing man ). Or the "Hey, You!" Haymaker . Or biting them . Or using Weighted Gloves . Or a Groin Attack . Or hitting the other guy upside the head with the hilt of their sword . Or bringing a bazooka to a sword fight . Or... well, you get the idea. They may adhere to Appropriate Levels of Violence simply to keep their enemies from escalation. Their pragmatic approach may justify why Power Does Not Equal Victory . That being said, however, there are different levels to combat pragmatism, as most still have some standards ; more heroic or sympathetic ones often limit their trickery and dirty fighting to direct combat situations, and even then they won't take it too far - not hurting civilians, for example . These kind of Combat Pragmatists are also rarely hypocritical, often being amused and impressed by an opponent's underhanded tactics to overcome them unless they cross a line they wouldn't. On the other hand, more unscrupulous or villainous pragmatists are willing to do literally everything to win a fight, up and including feigning surrender , using Innocent Bystanders as hostages or meatshields when they are not acceptable casualties to a brutal Blast Out trying to catch the heroes with their pants down (heroes think there is Safety in Muggles … nope), or even brutally maiming those who already surrendered ; often, this is used to show how much of a bastard the character is, or underline them being a Dirty Coward who would not react as well when the shoe is on the other foot . The comparative strength of the opponents also is a factor on how well this trope is received; a weaker character using every trick in the book against an opponent that significantly outclasses them may be considered entirely reasonable or even admirable in an unconventional sort of way note It doesn't matter if it's a hero or villain doing so - the Villainous Underdog often gets props from the viewer for facing up against a hero who would outclass them by any normal standard . In contrast, fighting dirty against an opponent on your level or weaker is often considered bad form and a sign of cowardice, insecurity or smugness , considering they could've just as easily won fair and square . On occasion, a Badass Teacher or Cynical Mentor will be this when teaching their mentee to fight, particularly if the mentee is inexperienced, is being trained to literally fight for their life, or at some other extreme disadvantage to 'honorable' opponents. This is more common in literature where fighting is more realistic, so it's harder to downplay a blow received and it's difficult to describe an 'honorable' fight where a disadvantaged opponent would realistically win. Often overlaps with the No-Nonsense Nemesis , Anti Heroes , and especially Ninja . They almost never allow honor or sentiment to cloud their judgement, will usually take measures to avoid setbacks, or betrayal . Sometimes Weak, but Skilled or Too Clever by Half . It can look like Confusion Fu in practice — but it almost always isn't. If The Unfettered fights, then they'll be one of these. In a video game, don't expect them to be above using a Cheese Strategy to win. See also Pragmatic Villainy . Contrast Dick Dastardly Stops to Cheat , which is playing dirty even when it would hinder your chance to actually win. Not to be confused with Why Don't You Just Shoot Him? (although often is a subversion of that trope).
